speaker: interviewer
text: It's July 28, 1994, talking to Agnes Kun. Number four, right? OK, so what happened after you were liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were liberated on April 14. The war was still going on, obviously. We did stay in the camp for a couple more weeks. And the French POWs, a few of them, did take care of us. Because they knew where to get supplies, because everybody-- everything was still sort of chaotic. And there wasn't a permanent American administration or anything. They took care of us. They got supplies for us. And eventually we were moved to a former, former German air base, about two kilometers outside of the town, where we had brick, two-story brick buildings with hot showers. And we had all the soap we could-- and it was just a totally different life. I mean, it was wonderful. It was a DP camp. There were about 18,000 to 20,000 people, from all over Germany. Not just Jews, all kinds, including, even, I guess, some Hungarian and other fascists who happened to be in Germany at that time. I started to work pretty soon, in one of the mess halls, where we fed about 5,000 people, as an interpreter. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Of course, this was all volunteer work. It was unpaid work. But we were happy to do anything for our liberators. Very soon, of course, the war ended on May 8 of 1945. We started getting lists that were compiled from other camps. And of course, I was looking for my father's name on every one of those, because I knew he left Auschwitz. And I just knew that he's somewhere, waiting for me. Of course, I didn't see his name on any of those lists. But I had a million excuses. He left earlier, he was in a smaller camp, they didn't have a list. I just didn't want to believe, I guess, I didn't want to believe it that I'm all alone. Eventually, that part was taken over by the British. And then it was given to the Russians. It was East Germany until recently-- the unification. And we joined a-- there were some-- we met some women who were also from Hungary, but from the former Yugoslavia. Because a portion of Yugoslavia was also given back to Hungary in-- during Second World War. And we-- through them, we attached ourselves to a Yugoslavian POW group. And we came home with them. We came home through Prague. A lot of-- all the western nations sent buses for their people, immediately, practically, after the war. The Czechs, from Czechoslovakia, they sent buses for their people, even though they didn't deport them. They had nothing to do with it, because it was the Hungarians who did it, from the territories were-- that belonged to Hungary. I guess only the Hungarians didn't give a damn. They didn't care if we ever returned or not. They didn't send anything, so we were on our own. I got back in my hometown-- there was also a big difference when we arrived to Prague. The Czechs were just, you know, wonderful. For that one day we were there, we were given food, and we were given some money. And we traveled on public transportation. Nobody asked us to buy a ticket, because we didn't have anything. We got to Budapest, we couldn't even get on a streetcar without paying. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Spent a few days in Budapest. And I guess I just want to say a little thing about that. My mother had some cousins. And I-- the men were all murdered by the, by the Hungarian Arrow Cross party. They were all shot in the back. Anyway, about three of the cousins. But the women were there. And I remember somebody was telling me about this old lady in her 80s who died. And they were so sad about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't say anything. But I just almost screamed, because I just-- you know, telling me that somebody in their 80s died, you know, in their bed? My parents were both in their 40s, and they were killed. I just, you know, couldn't even listen to it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got back to my hometown. And I found out very soon that my father was taken to Ebensee, which was in Austria. It was the subcamp of Mauthausen. They were building a tunnel for some secret factory. And very, very few people made it there, who were there for the duration. I heard from some people who were-- who got there only a couple of months before a liberation that my father died four to six weeks before the liberation, of overwork, starvation. It was terrible camp. I--later I read a book about it. It was one of the worst working camps. I guess I got home. I was alone. I think the most important lesson I learned-- when I was standing in the middle of my parents' empty house, everything that was movable was taken by the mob, by the Nazis, by the Russians, whoever got to it first. I think the most valuable lesson I learned was that the only thing worth crying for was human life. Nothing else really mattered. I stayed with-- only one of my girlfriends came back with her mother. And I stayed with them for a few weeks. And then I went to the city where my cousin was from. And I registered to the-- in the university there. They had a good university there. And I stayed there for one year. After that, I got married. In 1946, I got married. And And we had two daughters the first four years of our marriage. We stayed unwillingly in communist Romania, until 1961. Actually, we asked for a passport out when Marianne was three months old, I think-- maybe two-- on our first passport pictures. By the time the we left, she was 11. We came to the States in 1962. We stayed a year in Belgium, came to Los Angeles in 1963. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's about it. I have three granddaughters now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, before we have your daughter speak or show the photographs, is there anything else that you would just like to say about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I guess-- I will never forget, obviously. But hating the way we were hated would really put me to the same level as our murderers were. So besides that, today's Germany, most people were not even born when this happened, or they were very young. I could not have lived in Germany. We had an opportunity to do so, when we got out in the early '60s. But it was too early. I knew that I wouldn't be able to look at anybody over 40 without thinking that maybe he murdered my mother or my father. So I couldn't have lived in Germany at that point. Today, probably there are very few who survived, so it is not that same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: I guess that's about it, I think. Sometimes when I talk in the museum, people, sort of, maybe in a roundabout way, ask me, how can I be as normal as I am? I suppose losing parents is a horrible thing, especially at a young-- losing young parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the normal way of life is that parents do die before children. And I suppose those of us who lost children or spouses have even a harder time of it, forgetting, or being, quote, unquote, 'normal.' I guess when you're young and physically and mentally healthy, you are able to-- not get over it-- but to create, to create a normal life for yourself.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to--

speaker: interviewee
text: And what do I do?

speaker: interviewer
text: Get them at that speed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ok and then-- OK-- would you ask your daughter, Marianne, to join us for a minute?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marianne?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi, Mama.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was wondering if you could just tell us briefly how your mother's experiences have affected you as growing up, and particularly growing up in Eastern Europe and then coming to this country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think probably the one thing that I remember at an early age was realizing that when I was around 17, 18 was, as I was reaching that age, that that was when my mother went through these experiences. And although we weren't raised in a religious way as Jews, it made me feel very much Jewish, because this was a Jewish experience. And so it was my first sense of identifying with the Jewish people and with this cultural heritage and sadness that, I guess, pervades the, the Jews of Eastern Europe, who have experienced this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anything or much about it when you were growing up in Europe? Or did you learn most about it when you came here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think as children, we weren't sat down and told about it. But I think slowly there was more and more revealed to us about the experience. Probably not until I was a young adult did I start asking questions and feeling that my mom was somebody that I could find out more about her experiences. And, so it's-- it was, I don't know. Do you remember talking much about it when I was a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not, not Back in Romania. I don't-- I mean, you were only 11 when we left. So I don't really know. I guess you always knew about it. First of all, nobody had grandparents, you know. I mean, that was one thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't have grandparents. And none of their Jewish friends had-- one, Robbie. There was one grandmother, I guess-- had grandparents. And that made them a little different, I suppose.

speaker: interviewee
text: I suppose in, in the United States, right now I'm very proud of my mom being someone who speaks English well and was at an age where she could understand what was going on, back when she was 18 and is now willing and able to share her experiences with the younger generations and with non-Jews. And the talks that she gives, I think, are really part of history, and part of her life that hopefully none of us will ever know what she went through. But I think the recording that you're doing right now is a good way to preserve some of that and hopefully learn from the past and never repeat it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our oldest grandchild, Lora. She just turned 16. She is wonderful. Last night, we just saw her in a Tennessee William play, doing a great job. She is perfect. [LAUGHS] She's a real warm person. She can communicate with everybody, starting with age 2 through 86. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she's going to do something with her life that will be doing with people, I'm sure. This is Jamie, Lora's younger sister. She is 12, going to be 13 in October, getting ready for her bat mitzvah. She's a real doer, very bright. I wouldn't be surprised if she would do something science-oriented with her life. This is Natalie, our older daughter Annie's only child. She's going to be five next month. A nonstop talker, absolutely adorable. I would like to freeze her right here. But I'm sure she'll be wonderful as she grows up, too.
